item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmm. I downvoted this for almost the exact reasons why everyone else loved it. I'm not sure why the randomness bothers me. I get that it's essentially a broken magic ring, and I get that the article would in fact be worse if it was a perfectly functioning magic ring. But all I'm left with is a feeling of…. Bafflement? Usually I like me some bizarre, but this seems too much?
Maybe if the effects were a little more related, I would have enjoyed the concept, but as is I feel like it suffers from the contest that spawned it, which from context I assume required the inclusion of random words.
Regardless, it's a successful article. Just felt obliged to explain my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely love this! I'm reminded of playing Star Ocean's item creation. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: contact phenylalanine? the food additive? upvotes for dedication

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sheer redundancy applied to make the implied truism more explicit made that sentence a great start to a hilariously bizarre article. Loved it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would actually argue that it would be a weaker article if the effects did make sense. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this as a standalone, above and beyond the challenge the spawned it. The elements don't make sense, but that's fine. They don't need to make sense.

All of this is just to let us know that there is a very, very bad enchanter out there, discarding hazardous items from his previous projects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this! One man's trash is another man's treasure :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's for science. You monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It sounds very GLaDOS.

No, GLaDOS would have a more mocking tone in her words

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even on a re-read of that paragraph, I'm not getting that sense 100% - maybe add just a word or two here or there about how the conscious control is over movement, either of individual hairs or of locks of hair? It seems like that's something the foundation would quantify in a little more detail.

Everybody else seems to get that point, so maybe it's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually really like the sentence: "It is recommended that any human test subjects interacting with SCP-1385 should be screened for shellfish allergies, excluding tests involving SCP-1385 and subjects with shellfish allergies."

It sounds very GLaDOS.

Although, grammatically, you need to get rid of "should," it repeats the word "recommended."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Likewise. I didn't even see the title. i need to start checking these things in the mainlist after catching them in recently created. I just pictured it as a kind of DnD-type rod of wonder. Random stuff for comedy of errors. (if you're a DnD player, and haven'rt used a rod of wonder, Go play now and use one. Chaos will ensue, and fun will be had.)

Anyways, I've stewed on this overnight and decided that i like it, so +1ing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like me. I seem to assume everything is incredibly complicated. But I'm happy I'm wrong this time, because the real answer is infinitely more funny than mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was at least under the impression that not many people were getting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, did so many people not get it? I thought that much was trivially obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By "conscious control", I did indeed mean it can move. It's like having a functional net that's a part of your body. The one semi-useful effect to come out of this ring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: DING DING DING DING DING! Ladies and gentlemen, we have a winner. I thought people would have figured it out a lot sooner, given it's called "The Mistake Ring".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

Wow. That makes a lot of sense. A whole lot. Christ, I was going to start breaking out my books on Arthurian legend and re-listen to Wagner's Ring Cycle because I am…I think I need help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly what I came to post! Random screw ups are sometimes the most frustrating. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So a guy tried to make a magic conjuring ring, and he did it wrong? If I'm reading it right, this thing is the magitek equivalent of that one computer your friend tried to fix — you know, the one that boots straight into Windows 3.1 every time and won't stop beeping unless you unplug it (and sometimes not even then). It's random and bizarre because it's a failure, a mistake made by someone juuuust educated enough to screw things up REALLY spectacularly.

I love it. +1, especially because it made me LOL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Formatting of the species name of the thistle was perfect in terms of use of italics and capitalization. However, italics are not used above the genus level. Thus, families are capitalized but not italicized. I made the edit already.

I think Mjölnir ought to be named in the article with "Thor's hammer" in the footnotes or in parentheses, but that's a stylistic choice I'll leave to the author's discretion.

As for the SCP itself: the journal entries were just long enough to have interesting details without being close to overly long. Maggots in the earwax is horrifying and my favorite touch in the whole thing.

The effects of this particular ring are… weird. I liked the thistle bit and in my mind it was some weird religious artifact created to punish sinners or some such, but then the hair thing came up. What does it mean that the wearer has "conscious control" over the hair? Can the hair move? Is it a functional net? Or can the wearer just direct the growth?

Even with the cool addendum sort of explaining it, the weird mix of three effects on the ring, none of which is described quite enough in detail, is enough for me to withhold a vote either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my, I'm sorry, I forgot to mention that I liked this anyway. 1+ (I actually upvoted before the comment, I merely forgot to mention it here. Useless memory…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't worry about it. There is some hidden depth, but it has nothing to do with any mythological things. I'd look at a few of my other works for clues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what would be nice, a tale showing exactly how this guy is getting so frustrated, it doesn't have to say what he's trying to do, but I just wanna know more about this guy and the moments leading to his rage quit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always do that, can't help myself. My apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're reading more into this than there actually is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm. The Ring, thistle and hair said Norse to me. But after a re-reading…is this, perchance, something mildly-Arthurian related?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now everything makes sense.

This definitely wouldn't have worked without the journal entries. But I just love the idea of a guy making tons of these rings and just continually, spectacularly failing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a bad route to start with when trying to figure this out, but Norse myth actually has very little to do with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, yes, time to submit my first SCP, with the number 1385-

FUCK IT!

Still, probably what I deserve.
On topic though… Meh. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have absurdly mixed feelings about this one. I absolutely love the writing. But the effects… I know that they're supposed to be random, but they're almost too random.

No vote while i muse on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's always facinating to see where your mind goes, Doc. You keep incentivizing me to study back up on my Norse mythology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Know how if you ask a plumber about what's wrong with your car, the first thing he looks at are the pipes? Well, I'm bout' to do that, replacing plumbing with Norse myth.

So lets see what we have. There's a concrete ring, very worn even if it is made of concrete, and its first and most obvious effect makes thistle shoot up forth' from the ground. Hm. Thistle, mistle, and all that bristles, heheh. And what is this? Locks of hair that grow and grow? I wonder how many are blondes..

Can't work in the shellfish though, damn it. Maybe Njord or Ægir are interested in something..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thoughts too. I liked the "feel" of the piece, and I couldn't explain to you what that means if I wanted to.

When're these motherfucking kids gonna show up so I can tutor them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very odd, but I did find myself enjoying it. The words you got from the challenge weren't exactly easy, so good job making something decent with them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture part is done. The rest I leave to your imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm curious to what was actually trying to be accomplished by making the ring.

And that white space in the picture is bothering me. At leadt crop it in MS paint or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very… weird. Yet enjoyable all the same. Well written, classically bizarre, have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm torn between liking the "unfinished" quality of the SCP itself, and disliking how off the wall its anomalous effects seem to be.

No vote for now

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that's kind of the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just doesn't feel coherent to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was looking more at the rounding of the edges.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's purely a coincidence, actually. I've never even watched the Disney movie all the way through, much less the original story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is an attempt at Dmatix's Challenge, using the terms ring, concrete, thistle, lobster, and net.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article does say it has a glossy cover…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… he summoned The Little Mermaid from the original Hans Christian Anderson story?

Also, that pic looks more like a rough-cast silver or other metallic ring, rather than one made of concrete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was intended to denote the frustration of the writer, but now that you mention it, it does seem irrelevant. Let me see if I can make it a little more clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have another issue;

Note: This entry was written in black marker, as opposed to the black pen used in all other entries

Can you make it more clear what that has to do with anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha, it's been so long since I've posted one I forgot you had to redo the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP is 100% capitalized just so you know
